source: CIS AWS END USER COMPUTE SERVICES BENCHMARK V1.2.0
title: Ensure MFA is enabled for WorkSpaces users

input_row.source: CIS AWS END USER COMPUTE SERVICES BENCHMARK V1.2.0
input_row.title: Ensure MFA is enabled for WorkSpaces users
input_row.description: Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA) adds an extra layer of authentication assurance beyond traditional username and password. With MFA enabled, when a user signs in to Amazon WorkSpaces, they will be prompted for their user name and password as well as for an authentication code from their physical or virtual MFA token. It is recommended that MFA be enabled for all accounts that utilize WorkSpaces.
input_row.rationale: Enabling MFA provides increased security to a username and password as it requires the user to have a virtual or physical hardware solution that displays a time-sensitive code.
input_row.audit: Preform the following steps to check multi-factor authentication is enabled for WorkSpaces From the Console: For AWS Managed AD Authenticated Amazon Workspaces Environments: 1. Identify the IP address of your RADIUS MFA server and your AWS Managed Microsoft AD directory. 2. In the AWS Directory Service console navigation pane, select Directories. 3. Choose the directory ID link for your AWS Managed Microsoft AD directory. 4. On the Directories page, scroll to the Multi-factor authentication section. 5. In the Multi-factor authentication section, confirm that it is enabled and that Radius Status is completed. For Self-Managed AD (with AD Connector) Amazon Workspaces Environments: 1. Identify the IP address and port of your RADIUS MFA server and your AWS Managed Microsoft AD Connector Identifier. 2. In the AWS Workspaces console navigation pane, select Directories. 3. Choose the directory ID link for your AWS Managed Microsoft AD connector. Internal Only - General 4. On the Directories page, select the Actions > Update Details. 5. In the Multi-factor authentication section, confirm that it is enabled and that Radius Status is completed, Enable Multi-Factor Authentication is checked and the IP Address of your Radius MFA server matches that of the RADIUS server IP address(es) field. If it is not enabled or the Radius status is in another state refer to the remediation steps below.
input_row.remediation: Perform the steps below to enable multi-factor authentication for WorkSpaces From the console: For AWS Managed Microsoft AD based Workspaces Environments: 1. Identify the IP address of your RADIUS MFA server and your AWS Managed Microsoft AD directory. 2. In the AWS Directory Service console navigation pane, select Directories. 3. Choose the directory ID link for your AWS Managed Microsoft AD directory. 4. On the Directories page, scroll to the Multi-factor authentication section. 5. In the Multi-factor authentication section, click Actions, and then click Enable. 6. On the Enable multi-factor authentication (MFA) page, provide the following values: • Display label - Provide a label name. • RADIUS server DNS name or IP addresses Note - AWS Directory Service does not support RADIUS Challenge/Response authentication. • Port - default 1812 • Shared secret code • Confirm shared secret code • Protocol - MS-CHAPv2 • Server timeout (in seconds) - 20 • Max RADIUS request retries - 3 *** Multi-factor authentication is available when the RADIUS Status changes to Enabled. 7. Click Enable. For AD Connector based Workspaces Environments: 1. Identify the IP address and port of your RADIUS MFA server and, your AWS Managed Microsoft AD Connector Identifier. 2. In the AWS Workspaces console navigation pane, select Directories. Internal Only - General 3. Choose the directory ID link for your AWS Managed Microsoft AD directory connector. 4. On the Directories page, scroll to the Multi-factor authentication section and select Edit. 5. In the Multi-factor authentication section, select Enable Multi-factor authentication. 6. On the Multi-Factor Authentication section, provide the following values: • RADIUS server DNS name (s) or IP address (s) • Port - default 1812 • Shared secret code • Confirm shared secret code • Protocol - MS-CHAPv2 • Server timeout (in seconds) - 20 • Max RADIUS request retries - 3 *** Multi-factor authentication is available when the RADIUS Status changes to Enabled. 7. Click Save
input_row.impact: To enable MFA for Amazon WorkSpaces you require a RADIUS server or a plugin to a RADIUS server already in use in your environment. Multi-factor authentication is not available for Simple AD.
input_row.justification: While the first opinion suggests automation is possible via AWS Directory Service APIs, this is technically inaccurate. The AWS Directory Service API does not expose per-user MFA status; MFA for WorkSpaces users is typically enforced via integration with Active Directory or a third-party IdP, and AWS APIs do not provide a method to enumerate or verify individual user MFA status. Therefore, verification requires manual inspection of the directory's MFA configuration and/or the IdP settings.
input_row.manual_summary: 1. Identify the directory (Simple AD, AD Connector, or AWS Managed Microsoft AD) associated with your AWS WorkSpaces deployment via the AWS Console: WorkSpaces > Directories. 2. Determine the authentication method in use: - If using AWS Managed Microsoft AD or AD Connector, MFA is typically enforced via RADIUS or integration with an IdP. - If using Simple AD, review the documentation for supported MFA options. 3. For RADIUS-based MFA: - In the AWS Console, go to Directory Service > Directories > [Your Directory] > Multi-Factor Authentication. - Confirm that MFA is enabled and configured (e.g., RADIUS server details are present and status is "Enabled"). 4. For SAML/IdP-based MFA: - Access your IdP (e.g., Azure AD, Okta, ADFS) management console. - Verify that MFA is enforced for all users/groups associated with AWS WorkSpaces. 5. Document the MFA configuration and capture evidence (screenshots or configuration exports) showing MFA enforcement. 6. Confirm compliance by ensuring all users who can access AWS WorkSpaces are subject to MFA at login.
input_row.default_value: By default, MFA is not enabled on AWS Workspaces. It requires a RADIUS server and an Active Directory environment.

step1_initial_assessment:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Manual

**MANUAL STEPS:**
1. **Identify your directory type**: 
   - Go to the AWS Management Console.
   - Navigate to the WorkSpaces service, and select "Directories".
   - Identify which type of directory (AWS Managed Microsoft AD, AD Connector, or Simple AD) is associated with your WorkSpaces deployment.

2. **Verify MFA settings for AWS Managed Microsoft AD WorkSpaces**:
   - Select the directory ID link for your AWS Managed Microsoft AD directory.
   - Scroll to the Multi-Factor Authentication section.
   - Verify that MFA is enabled. Check that RADIUS Status is "Enabled".

3. **Verify MFA settings for AD Connector WorkSpaces**:
   - Select the directory ID link for your AWS Managed Microsoft AD connector.
   - Confirm that Multi-factor authentication is enabled and configured: 
     - Verify IP Address of your RADIUS MFA server is listed.
     - Ensure that Enable Multi-Factor Authentication is checked.

4. **Check RADIUS configurations if applicable**:
   - For both AWS Managed Microsoft AD and AD Connector, cross-verify the listed RADIUS server DNS name or IP addresses, port, shared secret settings, and protocol (it should be MS-CHAPv2).
   - Ensure RADIUS status is "Enabled".

5. **For Simple AD environments**:
   - Review the AWS documentation to confirm if there are any supported MFA options since MFA is not natively available.

6. **Document your findings**:
   - Capture screenshots or record details of your MFA configuration settings confirming that MFA is enforced for all users who can access AWS WorkSpaces. 

7. **Confirm compliance**:
   - Check that all relevant users or user groups in your organization are subject to MFA when logging into AWS WorkSpaces via the managed directory service or the Identity Provider (IdP) if integrated.

By following these steps, you will have a manual verification of MFA settings for AWS WorkSpaces users.
step2_review:
**APPROACH VALIDATION:** PARTIALLY_AGREE

- **Manual vs Automated Classification:** The current assessment is classified as manual, which is partially correct. While some aspects of verifying MFA settings for WorkSpaces may require manual inspection, much of this can be automated using AWS APIs, boto3, or AWS CLI.
- **AWS APIs/boto3/CLI:** The AWS Directory Service and WorkSpaces APIs can be leveraged to automate the verification of MFA settings. Specifically, the `describe_directories` API call can be used to retrieve information about directory configurations, including MFA settings. The `boto3` library in Python can facilitate this automation.

**TECHNICAL ACCURACY:** 6

- **Automation Details:** The assessment lacks automation details, which could be addressed by incorporating boto3 or AWS CLI commands. For example, using `boto3.client('workspaces')` and `boto3.client('ds')` to programmatically check directory configurations.
- **Manual Steps:** The manual steps are generally accurate but could be more efficient with automation. The steps for checking RADIUS configurations are detailed but could benefit from additional guidance on using AWS CLI or boto3 for verification.
- **Program Naming Convention:** The assessment does not provide a program name. A suggested name could be `aws_workspaces_mfa_verification`.

**IMPROVEMENTS:**

- **Automation:** Incorporate AWS CLI commands or boto3 scripts to automate the verification process. For example:
  ```python
  import boto3

  client = boto3.client('ds')
  response = client.describe_directories()
  for directory in response['DirectoryDescriptions']:
      if directory['Type'] in ['MicrosoftAD', 'ADConnector']:
          print(f"Directory ID: {directory['DirectoryId']}, MFA Enabled: {directory['RadiusSettings']['RadiusStatus'] == 'Enabled'}")
  ```
- **Missing Considerations:** The assessment does not consider AWS Config rules, which can be used to continuously monitor and enforce MFA settings.
- **Alternative Methods:** Utilize AWS Config to set up rules that ensure MFA is enabled for WorkSpaces directories.
- **Program Name Suggestion:** `aws_workspaces_mfa_status_check`

**CONFIDENCE:** MEDIUM

- The manual steps are well-documented, but the lack of automation options reduces confidence in the completeness of the assessment. Incorporating AWS API capabilities would increase confidence.

**FINAL RECOMMENDATION:**

- **Modify:** Enhance the assessment by integrating AWS CLI and boto3 automation options. Provide examples of how to use these tools to verify MFA settings programmatically. Additionally, consider using AWS Config to automate compliance checks.
- **Re-classify:** Consider re-classifying this assessment as a combination of manual and automated, given the potential for automation using AWS services.

step3_final_decision.decision_text:
**FINAL APPROACH:** Automated

**JUSTIFICATION:**
The verification of MFA settings for Amazon WorkSpaces can be automated using AWS APIs. The `describe_directories` API call in the AWS Directory Service can be used to retrieve directory configurations, including MFA settings. This allows for programmatic checks of whether MFA is enabled, making the process more efficient and less prone to human error compared to manual inspection.

**CONFIDENCE:** HIGH

---

**PROGRAM NAME:** 
aws_workspaces_mfa_verification

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:**
- AWS Resource/Service: AWS Directory Service, Amazon WorkSpaces
- API/CLI Method: `boto3.client('ds').describe_directories()`
- Properties to Check: `RadiusSettings.Status`, `RadiusSettings.RadiusServers`
- Pass Criteria: `RadiusSettings.Status` is "Enabled" and `RadiusSettings.RadiusServers` contains valid IP addresses.
- Fail Criteria: `RadiusSettings.Status` is not "Enabled" or `RadiusSettings.RadiusServers` is empty or incorrect.

---

**DISAGREEMENTS RESOLVED:**
The first AI opinion suggested a manual approach, while the second AI opinion highlighted the potential for automation using AWS APIs. Given the capabilities of AWS APIs to retrieve and verify directory configurations programmatically, the automated approach is more efficient and reliable.

**SOURCE:** CIS AWS END USER COMPUTE SERVICES BENCHMARK V1.2.0
